text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

i's Matt Butler watched the Olympics at a fan zone outside the Stade de France where spectators are actually treated like adults

Have you heard the one about the Irishman, the German and the person dressed as a republican hat?

It may sound like a dodgy joke but whenivisited the fan zone near the Stade de France, it made perfect sense. Far more sense (and so much cheaper) than the options for food and drink at theOlympicvenues.

Inside the places where the sporting action is, the options on offer are grim (more on them later). ButParis 2024organisers have set up 18 fan zones throughout the city, from the ancient Roman arena L’Arene de Lutece to theParc Rives de Seineon the swanky left bank. And they are a melting pot of people there simply to enjoy the Games.

They feature bars, food trucks and an enormous big screen on which various Olympic disciplines are broadcast non-stop. French medallists also stop by.

Oni’s visit to a tiny park on the banks of the Saint-Denis Canal near the A1 which roars past the Stade de France (it was nicer than it sounds), the mountain bike gold medallist Pauline Ferrand-Prevot appeared for a short on-stage interview and half an hour of selfies and autographs.

There were intermittent chants of “Paul-ine, Paul-ine, Paul-ine” throughout her appearance on stage.

French fans were enraptured by Ferrand-Prevot and they were by far in the majority at the surprisingly picturesque location in a rather unlovely part of Paris, but there was also Dutch, Irish, Italian and New Zealand livery on view on the fan’s clothes and accessories.

The “New Zealanders” turned out to be a German couple from Cologne who had only travelled five hours by car instead of the 30 or so in an aeroplane that they’d have to have endured had they come from the land of the long white cloud, but their dedication to the Kiwis was admirable.

They had not long returned from Lyon, where they had watched New Zealand lose 2-1 to the hosts on Wednesday in the football.

An Irish couple – the man’s name was Keith, but the woman didn’t reveal hers – were thoroughly enjoying their time in Paris.

“It is amazing, everywhere we’ve gone has been great,” she said. “We haven’t actually seen any events yet, but it has been great.”

They generously offered to hold my belongings and take my picture with the Phryge mascot, after the long queue of children had had their selfie with the anthropomorphic revolutionary hat.

The big screen showed a direct feed of action with extremely loud French commentary. And the action was tailored to the local audience. The fencing team semi-finals between France and Hungary was broadcast until the hosts’ fortunes took a dive.

It swiftly switched to judo and the women’s super-heavyweight semi-final bout between Romane Dicko of France and Brazil’s Beatriz Sousa. The home athlete lost.Zut alors.

Thankfully 35-year-old judo super-heavyweight veteran Teddy Riner obliged with a victory in his semi-final, then the final against South Korea’s Kim Min-jong. A huge roar went up from the amassed fans lounging in Paris 2024-coloured deckchairs. Judo is big here.

The refreshment options were wide. We had burritos, crepes, Peruvian sausage and burgers to choose from – and none were far above the prohibitive prices that Parisian nourishment tends to be (ham and cheese on toast, with a fancy name, commands a hefty tariff of up to 16 euros in the swanky centre).

A decent burger and chips – think trendy “smash” sandwiches and gastropub French fries, rather than McDonald’s – was 14 euros. There was also an enticing vegan option, complete with a French touch. The burger van had an option curated by none other than Alain Ducasse, at a euro less than the beef choice. Sadly it had sold out – which is probably a good sign.

In contrast, IOC-approved venue kiosks at the Stade de France offered spartan, tired looking sandwiches at 8.50 euros each, mystery-meat hot dogs for 9.50 or an “egg muffin” (one of only two vegetarian options) at eight euros. Some places had a slice of stodgy looking quiche at the same price. The only thing vegans can eat at venues is fries.

Drinks included champagne, of course, (12 euros a glass or 70 a bottle) and beer, which was served in 480ml cups at seven euros a pop.

Again, not far above the prices in bars outside the Olympic bubble. The bartender confirmed that champagne sales had been steady. And unlike the concessions inside venues, the beer and wine actually contained alcohol.

In venues, the only choice apart from water and Coca-Cola brands is zero per cent Kronenbourg at eight euros for half a litre. Venues do, however, have free water stations for people to fill up bottles they bring.

Yes, alcohol is not the be-all-and-end-all at sporting events, but it would be nice for the IOC to treat their fans like adults – and cater to people’s choices that go beyond overpriced, beige, wheat-based food options. Bravo Paris 2024 for providing this for the hoi polloi who do not have tickets, or who wish to relax in the sun before heading to the events they have paid handsomely for. For the IOC, the message is “doit faire mieux” – must do better.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: Inside the Paris 2024 fan zone shaming ‘grim’ Olympic venues
author: Matt Butler
description: i's Matt Butler watched the Olympics at a fan zone outside the Stade de France where spectators are actually treated like adults
